splits.legalQuestion: Whether the rule of lenity trumps Auer/Kisor deference to agency interpretations of ambiguous provisions in the context of criminal sentencing guidelines.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether courts should defer to the Sentencing Commission's anti-defendant interpretations of ambiguous Guidelines provisions or instead apply the rule of lenity in favor of the defendant. This matters because it determines whether agencies can effectively expand criminal liability through interpretive commentary.

positions.label: Lenity trumps deference
positions.summary: Holds that the rule of lenity takes precedence over Auer/Kisor deference when interpreting ambiguous criminal sentencing provisions, preventing agencies from using delegated power to expand criminal liability.

positions.label: Deference trumps lenity
positions.summary: Holds that agency deference (Auer/Kisor) to the Sentencing Commission's commentary takes precedence over the rule of lenity when interpreting ambiguous Guidelines provisions.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Sentencing Guidelines commentary (Application Note 1) that adds inchoate offenses like conspiracy to the definition of 'controlled substance offense' in U.S.S.G. § 4B1.2(b) is entitled to deference under Stinson/Kisor when the guideline text does not mention inchoate offenses.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether Application Note 1's expansion of 'controlled substance offense' to include conspiracy and other inchoate offenses is a valid, deferrable interpretation of an ambiguous guideline or an impermissible expansion beyond the guideline's plain text. This significantly affects career-offender designations and sentences.

positions.label: Commentary is entitled to deference
positions.summary: Holds that Application Note 1 is the Sentencing Commission's authoritative interpretation of an ambiguous guideline term and is entitled to controlling weight, so inchoate offenses like conspiracy qualify as 'controlled substance offenses.'

positions.label: Commentary exceeds guideline text
positions.summary: Holds that the guideline text unambiguously excludes inchoate offenses, so Application Note 1 impermissibly expands the definition and is not entitled to deference.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the word 'and' in the First Step Act's safety-valve provision, 18 U.S.C. § 3553(f)(1), is conjunctive (requiring all three criminal-history criteria to disqualify a defendant) or disjunctive (requiring only one criterion to disqualify).
splits.description: Circuits are deeply split on whether a defendant must meet all three criminal-history criteria in § 3553(f)(1) to be disqualified from safety-valve relief, or whether meeting any one criterion is disqualifying. This affects mandatory minimum sentencing for drug offenses and has produced extensive disagreement among circuit judges.

positions.label: 'And' is conjunctive (all three required)
positions.summary: Holds that defendants are disqualified from safety-valve relief only if they meet all three criminal-history criteria listed in § 3553(f)(1), reading 'and' in its ordinary conjunctive sense.

positions.label: 'And' is disjunctive (any one suffices)
positions.summary: Holds that possessing any one of the three criminal-history criteria in § 3553(f)(1) is sufficient to disqualify a defendant from safety-valve relief.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a state drug conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' under the Sentencing Guidelines even if the substance is not controlled under federal law (the Controlled Substances Act).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether the Guidelines' definition of 'controlled substance offense' encompasses state convictions for substances not on the federal drug schedules, or whether there must be a match between the state and federal controlled substance lists. This affects career-offender and other sentencing enhancements.

positions.label: State schedule controls (no federal match required)
positions.summary: Holds that the Guidelines broadly define 'controlled substance offense' to include any offense under state law, without requiring that the particular substance also be controlled under the federal Controlled Substances Act.

positions.label: Federal schedule match required
positions.summary: Holds that a state drug conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' under the Guidelines only if the substance at issue is also controlled under the federal Controlled Substances Act.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether non-retroactive changes in sentencing law, such as the First Step Act's anti-stacking amendment to 18 U.S.C. § 924(c), can constitute or contribute to 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether district courts may consider non-retroactive changes in law as a factor supporting compassionate release. Some circuits bar any reliance on such changes, while others permit them as part of a holistic analysis. The Sentencing Commission's 2023 amendment (§ 1B1.13(b)(6)) attempted to authorize such consideration, but its authority to do so is itself disputed.

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes cannot support compassionate release
positions.summary: Holds that a non-retroactive change in sentencing law, whether offered alone or in combination with other factors, cannot contribute to a finding of 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' for a sentence reduction under § 3582(c)(1)(A).

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes may support compassionate release
positions.summary: Holds that non-retroactive changes in law may be considered as one factor among others when evaluating whether extraordinary and compelling reasons warrant a sentence reduction under § 3582(c)(1)(A).

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a district court may expressly impose a sentence upon revocation of supervised release to punish a defendant for the violation of supervised release conditions, or only to account for the breach of trust and the statutory factors in 18 U.S.C. § 3553(a).
splits.description: The Sixth Circuit allows district courts to expressly punish defendants for supervised release violations, placing it at the 'extreme' of a circuit split. Other circuits hold that revocation sentences should be based on the breach of trust with the court rather than punishment for the new misconduct. This affects hundreds of revocation sentences annually.

positions.label: Punishment for the violation is permissible
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may expressly punish defendants for violations of supervised release when imposing revocation sentences.

positions.label: Punishment for the violation is not permissible
positions.summary: Holds that revocation sentences should reflect the breach of the defendant's trust with the court, not punish the defendant for the underlying violation conduct.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the civil appeal deadline or the criminal appeal deadline applies to an appeal from an order denying attorney's fees under the Hyde Amendment, which authorizes fee recovery in criminal cases where the prosecution was frivolous, vexatious, or in bad faith.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether a Hyde Amendment fee motion is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case (subject to 30-day civil deadline) or part of the criminal case itself (subject to 14-day criminal deadline). The Seventh Circuit joined the majority view that the civil deadline applies.

positions.label: Civil deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case, so the 30-day civil appeal deadline under Federal Rule of Appellate Procedure 4(a) applies.

positions.label: Criminal deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment motion is part of the criminal case, so the shorter 14-day criminal appeal deadline under Federal Rule of Appellate Procedure 4(b) applies.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether post-petition, pre-conversion increases in the equity of a debtor's asset belong to the bankruptcy estate or to the debtor upon good-faith conversion from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7 under 11 U.S.C. § 348(f)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether property appreciation that occurs between the filing of a Chapter 13 petition and conversion to Chapter 7 inures to the bankruptcy estate or remains with the debtor. This has major practical implications for debtors whose homes appreciate in value during their Chapter 13 cases.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the estate
positions.summary: Holds that the plain language of § 348(f)(1)(A), combined with the broad definition of 'property of the estate' in § 541(a), means post-petition appreciation inures to the bankruptcy estate upon conversion.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the debtor
positions.summary: Holds that the Bankruptcy Code's structure, object, policies, and legislative history establish that post-petition, pre-conversion appreciation belongs to the debtor, not the estate.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether an order denying a motion to seal documents containing medical treatment and diagnosis information is immediately appealable under the collateral order doctrine as 'effectively unreviewable on appeal from the final judgment.'
splits.description: The D.C. Circuit held that medical privacy interests are of sufficiently 'high order' to warrant immediate appellate review of orders denying sealing, recognizing that delayed review risks the very disclosure the seal was meant to prevent. Other circuits have not uniformly recognized this category of interlocutory appeal, creating potential divergence.

positions.label: Immediately appealable
positions.summary: Holds that maintaining one's privacy in medical treatments and diagnoses is a value of 'high order' that warrants immediate appellate review under the collateral order doctrine because the privacy interest cannot be adequately vindicated after final judgment.

positions.label: Not immediately appealable
positions.summary: Holds that orders denying motions to seal are not immediately appealable under the collateral order doctrine because other procedural mechanisms can vindicate the privacy interest.
